title: Rubella
oneLiner: Self-limiting febrile rash illness caused by rubivirus; critical to recognise due to catastrophic congenital infection; now rare in UK thanks to MMR.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Congenital rubella syndrome** (in pregnant patient, first trimester) – life-threatening foetal consequences. The single finding: **positive pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age with rash + fever.
- **Rubella encephalitis** – rare but can progress rapidly. Finding: **altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurology** accompanying the rash.
- **Severe thrombocytopenic purpura** – can cause fatal intracranial or GI bleeding. Finding: **petechiae/bruising outside rash areas + platelet count <20**.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: low-grade fever, malaise, headache, mild conjunctivitis (1–5 days)
  - Rash: pink maculopapular, starts on face and neck, spreads caudally, fades after 3 days (often first sign noticed)
  - Lymphadenopathy: post-auricular, suboccipital, posterior cervical (often precedes rash)
  - Arthralgia/arthritis: common in adult women (small joints, hands, wrists)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Rash (non-confluent, non-pruritic, may vanish within 24 hours) – use a glass diascopy: non-blanching suggests thrombocytopenia
  - Palpable tender lymph nodes behind ears/occiput
  - Mild conjunctivitis, but no Koplik spots (unlike measles)
  - In pregnancy: foetal ultrasound if late presentation (but too late for first-trimester intervention)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Measles** – higher fever (≥39°C), Koplik spots, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis. **Splitter: presence of post-auricular lymphadenopathy (rubella) vs Koplik spots (measles)**
- **Scarlet fever** – sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, pharyngitis, group A strep on throat swab
- **Parvovirus B19 (slapped cheek)** – classic erythema infectiosum in children; arthralgia in adults; reticulocytopenia in immunosuppressed
- **Enterovirus (echo/coxsackie)** – often hand-foot-and-mouth (vesicles), or other viral prodrome; fewer posterior nodes
- **Drug rash** – history of recent antibiotic/NSAID; often morbilliform, no lymphadenopathy; resolves on drug removal
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV)** – exudative pharyngitis, generalised lymphadenopathy, positive Monospot or serology
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Pregnancy test (urine β-hCG)** – mandatory in all females 12–55 years (even if says not pregnant – confirm)
  - Throat swab for rubella PCR (public health confirmation)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check platelets; anaemia rare)
  - Rubella serology: **IgM + IgG** – send urgent; local lab can usually run same day. Positive IgM suggests recent/active infection. IgG positive indicates past infection or vaccination.
  - Consider measles IgM/IgG if unsure (dual request)
  - LFTs (mild transaminitis common)
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed. CXR only if respiratory symptoms to exclude measles pneumonia.
- **Special tests:**
  - Rubella RNA detection by PCR (throat swab, urine, CSF) for confirmation and genotyping – not needed for acute management; for public health
  - Avidity testing on IgG if indeterminate serology (e.g. in pregnant women with positive IgM)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: acute onset of maculopapular rash + fever + lymphadenopathy. Confirmed by serology (IgM positive or fourfold IgG rise in paired sera)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – omission could lead to catastrophic congenital infection being missed until after first trimester.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Isolate the patient: **droplet precautions** (surgical mask for patient, staff in mask and gloves)
  - Notify local Health Protection Team (HP Team) by telephone – rubella is NOTIFIABLE in UK (statutory requirement)
  - Confirm pregnancy status – if pregnant, urgent obstetric referral (same day)
  - Supportive care: paracetamol for fever/malaise; oral fluids if tolerating
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Most patients do not need admission – discharge once diagnosis confirmed and not pregnant
  - If admitted (e.g. encephalitis, severe thrombocytopenia, pregnant for monitoring): continue isolation until 7 days after rash onset
  - Monitor for complications: daily check of neurological status, platelet count, pregnancy symptoms
  - For arthralgia: simple analgesia, NSAIDs if no contraindication
  - Pregnant women: urgent discussion with obstetric and foetal medicine team about risk of congenital rubella syndrome; offer counselling and consider termination
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Confirm seroconversion with convalescent serology (IgG at 2–4 weeks) if needed for public health or pregnancy follow-up
  - Post-exposure prophylaxis for contacts: MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure for non-immune contacts (not for pregnant or immunocompromised – offer human normal immunoglobulin (HNIG) if high risk, check local policy)
  - Record as "confirmed rubella" in GP notes to flag immunity status
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** if unable to tolerate oral – 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate (no specific rubella requirement)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin according to local protocol – check platelets before starting (thrombocytopenia may be relative contraindication)
  - **Diet:** NBM only if encephalitic and GCS <15; otherwise free oral intake
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2, more frequent if encephalitis (1–2 hourly)
  - **Isolation:** droplet precautions sign on door
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - No specific antiviral. Treatment purely supportive.
  - Paracetamol: 500 mg–1 g PO/IV 4–6 hourly (max 4 g/24h); reduce dose in hepatic impairment
  - Ibuprofen: 200–400 mg PO three times daily with food (for arthralgia); caution in thrombocytopenia/bleeding
- **Alternative for severe arthralgia:**
  - Naproxen 250–500 mg twice daily PO; or codeine 30–60 mg 4-hourly PRN if NSAID unsuitable
- **Post-exposure prophylaxis (not for active treatment):**
  - MMR vaccine – live attenuated; contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression
  - HNIG (human normal immunoglobulin) – for unprotected pregnant women exposed to rubella; dose: [check local policy e.g. 750 mg IM (0.12–0.15 mL/kg)] – never invent dose
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in severe thrombocytopenia, active bleeding, GI ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma
  - MMR vaccine: do not give if patient is immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, high-dose steroids, chemotherapy) or pregnant
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: no dose reduction needed in renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease (reduce to 3 g/day)
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution if eGFR 30–60
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Not applicable – no therapeutic drug for rubella itself. However, **giving MMR vaccine to an immunosuppressed or pregnant patient** is a serious error (live vaccine can cause disseminated disease); always check pregnancy test and medication history.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Arthritis/arthralgia (up to 50% of adult females; usually resolves in 2 weeks)
  - Thrombocytopenia (5–10%; usually mild and self-limiting; severe <1% – risk of bleeding)
  - Encephalitis (1 in 5000 cases; high mortality ~20%)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Congenital rubella syndrome** (if maternal infection in first 16 weeks) – deafness, cataracts, congenital heart disease (PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis), microcephaly, intellectual disability, hepatosplenomegaly, purpura
  - Persistent arthralgia (rare; months)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent in post-natal rubella: full recovery in 3–5 days. Encephalitis carries 20% mortality and risk of neurological sequelae. Congenital rubella syndrome has high morbidity; 50% of infected infants will have permanent damage.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check pregnancy status in every female 12–55 years** – this single step prevents catastrophic missed diagnosis.
- **Notify public health immediately** – they will arrange contact tracing and post-exposure vaccination to prevent secondary cases.
- **If you are unsure if rash is rubella or measles, isolate and send serology for both** – both are notifiable; over-notify, never under-notify.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not assume a maculopapular rash is rubella without considering drug reaction** – check drug history carefully, especially antibiotics and NSAIDs.
- **Do not give MMR vaccine to an acutely febrile patient** – wait until full recovery; vaccine is for prophylaxis, not treatment.
- **Do not ignore mild petechiae** – always check platelet count; thrombocytopenia can be the presenting feature of rubella and can worsen.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Confirmed pregnancy – patient must attend obstetric assessment same day
  - Worsening headache, confusion, seizures (encephalitis)
  - Rash becomes non-blanching, bleeding from gums/nose, black stools (thrombocytopenia)
  - Joint pain restricts normal function for >1 week
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2–3 days to review serology results and ensure no complications
  - Public Health will call for contact vaccination; ensure patient aware of this call
  - If pregnant, follow-up with foetal medicine at 2–4 weeks for detailed anomaly scan
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / obstetric registrar immediately if:**
  - Pregnant woman with confirmed or suspected rubella (any trimester)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - GCS <15 or sudden deterioration (encephalitis)
  - Seizure
  - Platelets <20 with active bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, oral purpura)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising lactate
- **Call public health (HP Team) immediately if:**
  - Any suspected rubella case (even while awaiting serology) – notification is a legal duty in UK

rawMarkdown:
## Rubella
**One-liner:** Self-limiting febrile rash illness caused by rubivirus; critical to recognise due to catastrophic congenital infection; now rare in UK thanks to MMR.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Congenital rubella syndrome** (in pregnant patient, first trimester) – life-threatening foetal consequences. The single finding: **positive pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age with rash + fever.
- **Rubella encephalitis** – rare but can progress rapidly. Finding: **altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurology** accompanying the rash.
- **Severe thrombocytopenic purpura** – can cause fatal intracranial or GI bleeding. Finding: **petechiae/bruising outside rash areas + platelet count <20**.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: low-grade fever, malaise, headache, mild conjunctivitis (1–5 days)
  - Rash: pink maculopapular, starts on face and neck, spreads caudally, fades after 3 days (often first sign noticed)
  - Lymphadenopathy: post-auricular, suboccipital, posterior cervical (often precedes rash)
  - Arthralgia/arthritis: common in adult women (small joints, hands, wrists)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Rash (non-confluent, non-pruritic, may vanish within 24 hours) – use a glass diascopy: non-blanching suggests thrombocytopenia
  - Palpable tender lymph nodes behind ears/occiput
  - Mild conjunctivitis, but no Koplik spots (unlike measles)
  - In pregnancy: foetal ultrasound if late presentation (but too late for first-trimester intervention)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Measles** – higher fever (≥39°C), Koplik spots, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis. **Splitter: presence of post-auricular lymphadenopathy (rubella) vs Koplik spots (measles)**
- **Scarlet fever** – sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, pharyngitis, group A strep on throat swab
- **Parvovirus B19 (slapped cheek)** – classic erythema infectiosum in children; arthralgia in adults; reticulocytopenia in immunosuppressed
- **Enterovirus (echo/coxsackie)** – often hand-foot-and-mouth (vesicles), or other viral prodrome; fewer posterior nodes
- **Drug rash** – history of recent antibiotic/NSAID; often morbilliform, no lymphadenopathy; resolves on drug removal
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV)** – exudative pharyngitis, generalised lymphadenopathy, positive Monospot or serology

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Pregnancy test (urine β-hCG)** – mandatory in all females 12–55 years (even if says not pregnant – confirm)
  - Throat swab for rubella PCR (public health confirmation)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check platelets; anaemia rare)
  - Rubella serology: **IgM + IgG** – send urgent; local lab can usually run same day. Positive IgM suggests recent/active infection. IgG positive indicates past infection or vaccination.
  - Consider measles IgM/IgG if unsure (dual request)
  - LFTs (mild transaminitis common)
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed. CXR only if respiratory symptoms to exclude measles pneumonia.
- **Special tests:**
  - Rubella RNA detection by PCR (throat swab, urine, CSF) for confirmation and genotyping – not needed for acute management; for public health
  - Avidity testing on IgG if indeterminate serology (e.g. in pregnant women with positive IgM)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: acute onset of maculopapular rash + fever + lymphadenopathy. Confirmed by serology (IgM positive or fourfold IgG rise in paired sera)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – omission could lead to catastrophic congenital infection being missed until after first trimester.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Isolate the patient: **droplet precautions** (surgical mask for patient, staff in mask and gloves)
  - Notify local Health Protection Team (HP Team) by telephone – rubella is NOTIFIABLE in UK (statutory requirement)
  - Confirm pregnancy status – if pregnant, urgent obstetric referral (same day)
  - Supportive care: paracetamol for fever/malaise; oral fluids if tolerating
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Most patients do not need admission – discharge once diagnosis confirmed and not pregnant
  - If admitted (e.g. encephalitis, severe thrombocytopenia, pregnant for monitoring): continue isolation until 7 days after rash onset
  - Monitor for complications: daily check of neurological status, platelet count, pregnancy symptoms
  - For arthralgia: simple analgesia, NSAIDs if no contraindication
  - Pregnant women: urgent discussion with obstetric and foetal medicine team about risk of congenital rubella syndrome; offer counselling and consider termination
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Confirm seroconversion with convalescent serology (IgG at 2–4 weeks) if needed for public health or pregnancy follow-up
  - Post-exposure prophylaxis for contacts: MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure for non-immune contacts (not for pregnant or immunocompromised – offer human normal immunoglobulin (HNIG) if high risk, check local policy)
  - Record as "confirmed rubella" in GP notes to flag immunity status
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** if unable to tolerate oral – 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate (no specific rubella requirement)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin according to local protocol – check platelets before starting (thrombocytopenia may be relative contraindication)
  - **Diet:** NBM only if encephalitic and GCS <15; otherwise free oral intake
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly NEWS2, more frequent if encephalitis (1–2 hourly)
  - **Isolation:** droplet precautions sign on door

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - No specific antiviral. Treatment purely supportive.
  - Paracetamol: 500 mg–1 g PO/IV 4–6 hourly (max 4 g/24h); reduce dose in hepatic impairment
  - Ibuprofen: 200–400 mg PO three times daily with food (for arthralgia); caution in thrombocytopenia/bleeding
- **Alternative for severe arthralgia:**
  - Naproxen 250–500 mg twice daily PO; or codeine 30–60 mg 4-hourly PRN if NSAID unsuitable
- **Post-exposure prophylaxis (not for active treatment):**
  - MMR vaccine – live attenuated; contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression
  - HNIG (human normal immunoglobulin) – for unprotected pregnant women exposed to rubella; dose: [check local policy e.g. 750 mg IM (0.12–0.15 mL/kg)] – never invent dose
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid in severe thrombocytopenia, active bleeding, GI ulcer, severe renal impairment, asthma
  - MMR vaccine: do not give if patient is immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, high-dose steroids, chemotherapy) or pregnant
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: no dose reduction needed in renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease (reduce to 3 g/day)
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; use with caution if eGFR 30–60
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Not applicable – no therapeutic drug for rubella itself. However, **giving MMR vaccine to an immunosuppressed or pregnant patient** is a serious error (live vaccine can cause disseminated disease); always check pregnancy test and medication history.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Arthritis/arthralgia (up to 50% of adult females; usually resolves in 2 weeks)
  - Thrombocytopenia (5–10%; usually mild and self-limiting; severe <1% – risk of bleeding)
  - Encephalitis (1 in 5000 cases; high mortality ~20%)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Congenital rubella syndrome** (if maternal infection in first 16 weeks) – deafness, cataracts, congenital heart disease (PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis), microcephaly, intellectual disability, hepatosplenomegaly, purpura
  - Persistent arthralgia (rare; months)

### Prognosis
- Excellent in post-natal rubella: full recovery in 3–5 days. Encephalitis carries 20% mortality and risk of neurological sequelae. Congenital rubella syndrome has high morbidity; 50% of infected infants will have permanent damage.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check pregnancy status in every female 12–55 years** – this single step prevents catastrophic missed diagnosis.
- **Notify public health immediately** – they will arrange contact tracing and post-exposure vaccination to prevent secondary cases.
- **If you are unsure if rash is rubella or measles, isolate and send serology for both** – both are notifiable; over-notify, never under-notify.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not assume a maculopapular rash is rubella without considering drug reaction** – check drug history carefully, especially antibiotics and NSAIDs.
- **Do not give MMR vaccine to an acutely febrile patient** – wait until full recovery; vaccine is for prophylaxis, not treatment.
- **Do not ignore mild petechiae** – always check platelet count; thrombocytopenia can be the presenting feature of rubella and can worsen.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Confirmed pregnancy – patient must attend obstetric assessment same day
  - Worsening headache, confusion, seizures (encephalitis)
  - Rash becomes non-blanching, bleeding from gums/nose, black stools (thrombocytopenia)
  - Joint pain restricts normal function for >1 week
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2–3 days to review serology results and ensure no complications
  - Public Health will call for contact vaccination; ensure patient aware of this call
  - If pregnant, follow-up with foetal medicine at 2–4 weeks for detailed anomaly scan

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / obstetric registrar immediately if:**
  - Pregnant woman with confirmed or suspected rubella (any trimester)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - GCS <15 or sudden deterioration (encephalitis)
  - Seizure
  - Platelets <20 with active bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, oral purpura)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising lactate
- **Call public health (HP Team) immediately if:**
  - Any suspected rubella case (even while awaiting serology) – notification is a legal duty in UK
